items.q: How is economic growth shown on a Production Possibility Curve?
items.correct: By an outward shift of the entire curve — reflecting an increase in productive capacity through more or better resources, technology, or skills.
items.misconception: Growth is shown by moving from inside the curve to a point on the curve.
items.random_wrong: Growth is shown by the curve becoming straighter.
items.why: Moving from inside to on the curve is RECOVERY (using existing resources better), not growth. True growth requires the curve to shift outward — students confuse recovery from inefficiency with new capacity.

items.correct: A sustained rise in the general price level over time — reducing the purchasing power of money. Measured by the percentage change in a price index.
items.misconception: Inflation is when prices are high.
items.random_wrong: Inflation is when the economy grows too fast.
items.why: Inflation is about RISING prices, not high prices. A country can have stable high prices (no inflation) or rising low prices (high inflation). Students confuse level with rate of change.

items.q: What is the difference between fixed and variable costs?
items.correct: Fixed costs don't change with output (e.g. rent, salaries); variable costs change directly with output (e.g. raw materials, hourly wages).
items.misconception: Fixed costs never change at all, ever.
items.random_wrong: Fixed costs are paid once and variable costs are paid daily.
items.why: Fixed costs are fixed WITH RESPECT TO OUTPUT — they can change for other reasons (rent goes up). The distinction is whether producing more or less changes the cost.

items.q: Which is the standard definition of a RECESSION?
items.correct: Two consecutive quarters of falling real GDP
items.why: Correct answer: Two consecutive quarters of falling real GDP. “GDP fell for one month” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: GDP per capita is best for measuring?
items.why: Correct answer: Average living standards. “Total country size only” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: GDP = £2,400bn, population = 60 million. GDP per capita?
items.why: Exact answer: 40000. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
